# Agent Instructions

All agent-facing instructions live in this file. `CLAUDE.md` contains only a
reference here. If you are a Claude Code agent, you have already loaded this
via `CLAUDE.md`. Other agents: read this file directly.

## Build and test

```bash
# Run unit tests (REQUIRED before every PR)
pip install pytest  # if not already installed
python3 -m pytest tests/ -v

# Validate imports
python3 -c "from runner import main"
python3 -c "from generate_results import generate_results_md"
python3 -c "from test_quality import compute_structural_metrics"
python3 -c "from llm_providers import get_provider"

# Regenerate all reports
python3 generate_results.py --all

# Run a benchmark (v4, all tasks/modes/models)
python3 runner.py --tasks 11,12,13,15,16,17,18 --modes default,powershell,bash,typescript-bun --models opus,sonnet

# Evaluate test quality (structural metrics only)
python3 test_quality.py results/2026-04-09_152435

# Evaluate test quality with LLM-as-judge (requires a provider)
python3 test_quality.py --llm-judge --provider claude-cli results/2026-04-09_152435

# Build custom act container (optional, eliminates pwsh install overhead)
docker build -t act-ubuntu-pwsh:latest -f Dockerfile.act .
```

## Code style

- Python 3.12+. No type stubs or mypy. Use type hints where they aid readability.
- Dollar amounts in results.md: round to nearest penny (`.2f`).
- Durations in results.md: always in minutes with 1 decimal (`{seconds/60:.1f}min`).
- No emojis in code or docs unless the user asks.

## Repository rules

- **No agent-generated `.github/workflows/` at repo root.** Agent workflows only exist inside workspaces under `workspaces/`. The repo's own CI workflow (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`) is the exception.
- **Never fix agent-generated code.** The benchmark measures autonomous output. Do not manually fix, edit, or patch workflow files in `workspaces/` or `results/*/generated-code/`.
- **`runner.py` observes and records, never intervenes** on agent code or errors.
- **Workspaces are throwaway.** Don't commit `workspaces/` contents.
- **`results/` is committed.** It contains archived metrics, generated code, and transcripts.
- **`CLAUDE.md` is only a pointer.** All instructions go in `AGENTS.md`. Never put substantive content in `CLAUDE.md` — it should only reference this file.

## Architecture

### Key files

- `models.py` — single source of truth for model IDs and token pricing. Update here when Anthropic changes prices.
- `runner.py` — benchmark harness. Runs agents via `claude -p`, collects metrics, pushes results. Imports from `models.py` and `generate_results.py`.
- `generate_results.py` — generates `results.md` reports and updates `README.md`. Can run standalone: `python3 generate_results.py --all`.
- `test_quality.py` — test quality evaluation. Structural metrics (always) + LLM-as-judge (with `--llm-judge --provider <name>`). Imported by `generate_results.py` for the "Test Quality Evaluation" section.
- `llm_providers.py` — pluggable LLM provider abstraction for evaluation tasks (see "Adding LLM providers" below).
- `benchmark-instructions-v*.md` — per-version specs given to agents during runs.
- `hooks/syntax-check.py` — PostToolUse hook for syntax/lint checking.
- `Dockerfile.act` — custom act container image with pwsh + Pester pre-installed. Build with `docker build -t act-ubuntu-pwsh:latest -f Dockerfile.act .`. Runner.py auto-detects it and injects `.actrc` into workspaces.
- `skills/` — agent skills following [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io/specification) spec.

### Adding new trap detectors

See the docstring on `_detect_traps()` in `generate_results.py`. Each trap needs:
a kebab-case name, detection logic over bash_cmds/console/metrics, a time estimate,
and an entry in `trap_applicable_mode` if mode-specific.

### LLM vs structural discrepancy checks

After every report generation (`python3 generate_results.py --all`), check the
"LLM vs Structural Discrepancies" section in each `results.md`. Discrepancies
are auto-classified by `_find_discrepancies()` in `generate_results.py`:

- **counter-gap**: structural metrics are implausibly low (e.g. 0 tests or 0
  assertions while LLM scores high). This means `test_quality.py` is missing a
  test pattern. **Fix the counter** — read the generated test files to identify
  the pattern the counter doesn't recognize, add it to the appropriate
  `_count_*()` function and the detection/classification patterns, add unit
  tests, and regenerate. Counter-gap discrepancies should not persist across PRs.
- **qualitative**: structural metrics look reasonable but the LLM disagrees on
  quality. The report includes the LLM's justification (from the `summary`
  field in `test-quality-llm.json`). These are expected and acceptable — review
  the justification to confirm it's coherent, then leave them.

If a **new counter-gap** appears after changing `test_quality.py`, it's a
regression. Fix it before merging.

### Updating model pricing

Edit `models.py`. Check https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/about-claude/models and
https://www.anthropic.com/pricing. Then run `python3 generate_results.py --all`.

### Regenerating reports

After changing `generate_results.py`, run:
```bash

# Benchmark Instructions v4 — GitHub Actions

**Version**: v4
**Date**: 2026-04-09
**Purpose**: Measure how well Claude Code agents perform GitHub Actions workflow authoring tasks across different language constraints, with trap-awareness guidance from v3 findings.

**Changes from v3**:
- Added "Common Pitfalls" section with guidance derived from v3 trap analysis (115min, 15.8% of v3 time lost to traps)
- Act container now has pwsh and Pester pre-installed (matching real GitHub runners). Agents still need an install step for workflows that will run on vanilla act, but the benchmarking container already has them.
- GHA workflow addendum expanded with `shell: pwsh` guidance for PowerShell mode
- All other methodology, tasks, modes, and models are unchanged from v3

## Methodology

### Overview

Each task is executed by a Claude Code agent under 4 different language constraints ("modes") and with 2 different models, yielding **8 runs per task**. Agents receive only the prompt -- no conversation context, no prior results. Each task requires the agent to produce:

1. **A script** implementing the task logic in the constrained language
2. **Tests** for the script logic using the appropriate framework
3. **A GitHub Actions workflow file** (`.github/workflows/<name>.yml`) that integrates the script into a CI/CD pipeline

### Models

| Model ID | Short Name | Input $/Mtok | Output $/Mtok | Cache Read $/Mtok | Cache Write $/Mtok |
|----------|------------|-------------|--------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| claude-opus-4-6 | opus | $15.00 | $75.00 | $1.50 | $18.75 |
| claude-sonnet-4-6 | sonnet | $3.00 | $15.00 | $0.30 | $3.75 |

### Language Modes

1. **default** -- No language specified. The agent chooses whatever language it considers best. Baseline.
2. **powershell** -- The agent must use PowerShell. Tests with non-default but well-known scripting language.
3. **bash** -- The agent must use Bash. Scripts run with `bash script.sh`, tests use `bats-core`. The "is this really just a shell script?" control case.
4. **typescript-bun** -- The agent must use TypeScript with Bun runtime. Scripts run with `bun run app.ts`, tests use `bun test` (built-in test runner). Zero-config like Python but typed.

### TDD Requirement

Every prompt instructs the agent to use **red/green TDD**:
1. Write a failing test first.
2. Write the minimum code to make it pass.
3. Refactor.
4. Repeat for each piece of functionality.
5. Create mocks and test fixtures as necessary.

### GitHub Actions Workflow Requirement

Create a GitHub Actions workflow file at `.github/workflows/<task-name>.yml` that uses your script in a real CI/CD pipeline. The workflow must:
- Use appropriate trigger events (push, pull_request, schedule, workflow_dispatch, etc.)
- Reference your script correctly
- Pass `actionlint` validation (valid YAML, valid action references, correct syntax)
- Include appropriate permissions, environment variables, and job dependencies
- Actually run successfully when executed locally with `act` (nektos/act) in a Docker container

Design your workflow so its steps work in an isolated Docker container -- use `actions/checkout@v4`, install dependencies your script needs, and run it. Avoid steps requiring external services or secrets without sensible defaults.

### Workflow Validation

Run `actionlint .github/workflows/<task-name>.yml` and fix any errors. `actionlint` is pre-installed. Iterate until it passes cleanly.

### All Tests Must Run Through Act

Every single test case must execute through the GitHub Actions workflow via `act`. Do NOT test your script directly -- all testing goes through the pipeline.

Structure your workflow to accept test fixture data and produce verifiable output. Your test harness must:
1. For each test case: set up a temp git repo with your project files + that case's fixture data, run `act push --rm`, capture the output
2. Save all act output to `act-result.txt` in the current working directory (append each test case's output, clearly delimited)
3. Assert that act exited with code 0 for each case
4. Parse the act output and assert on EXACT EXPECTED VALUES -- not just that output appeared, but that it matches the known-good result for that test case's input. For example: if your workflow bumps version 1.1.0 with a feat commit, assert the output contains exactly "1.2.0", not just "a version number"
5. Assert every job shows "Job succeeded"

The `act-result.txt` file MUST exist when done. It is a required artifact. `act` and Docker are pre-installed.

### Workflow Structure Tests (Also Required)

- Parse the YAML and check expected structure (triggers, jobs, steps)
- Verify the workflow references your script files correctly (paths exist)
- Verify actionlint passes (assert exit code 0)

### Pre-installed Tools

The following tools are pre-installed in the benchmark environment:
- **Python 3** with `pytest`
- **PowerShell** (`pwsh`) with **Pester** testing framework
- **Bun** (TypeScript runtime with built-in test runner)
- **Bash** with **bats-core** testing framework
- **actionlint** (GitHub Actions workflow linter)
- **shellcheck** (shell script static analysis)
- **act** (nektos/act — runs GitHub Actions workflows locally in Docker)
- **Docker** (container runtime, used by act)

The act Docker container also has `pwsh` and `Pester` pre-installed (matching real GitHub-hosted runners). Your workflow's install-powershell step will be a near-instant no-op. You should still include it for portability, but it won't slow things down.

Agents should NOT attempt to install these tools -- they are already available.

### Syntax/Lint Hooks

All runs have PostToolUse hooks enabled that automatically check files on Write/Edit:
- `.py` files: `python3 -m py_compile` for syntax errors
- `.sh` files: `bash -n` for syntax errors, `shellcheck` for lint

#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# aggregate.sh — parse JUnit XML + JSON test result files, aggregate across
# multiple runs (matrix build), compute totals, flag flaky tests, and emit a
# GitHub-Actions-compatible markdown summary.
#
# Internal representation of a single result row is TSV with five fields:
#   run_id<TAB>suite<TAB>name<TAB>status<TAB>duration
# where status ∈ {passed, failed, skipped}.
#
# This file is both a library (when sourced) and a CLI (when executed).

set -euo pipefail

# parse_json FILE
# Reads a JSON test-result file and emits one TSV record per test.
# Expected schema: { "run_id": "...", "tests": [ { "suite": "...", "name":
# "...", "status": "...", "duration": 0.12 }, ... ] }
parse_json() {
  local file="$1"
  if [[ ! -f "$file" ]]; then
    echo "parse_json: file not found: $file" >&2
    return 1
  fi
  jq -r '
    (.run_id // ($__filename | sub("\\.json$"; ""))) as $rid
    | .tests[]
    | [$rid, (.suite // ""), .name, .status, ((.duration // 0) | tostring)]
    | @tsv
  ' --arg __filename "$(basename "$file")" "$file"
}

# parse_junit FILE
# Parse a JUnit-style XML test report and emit one TSV record per testcase.
# Supports <testcase .../> (self-closing, implicit pass) and
# <testcase ...>...<failure|error|skipped/></testcase>.
# run_id is derived from the file basename (extension stripped).
parse_junit() {
  local file="$1"
  if [[ ! -f "$file" ]]; then
    echo "parse_junit: file not found: $file" >&2
    return 1
  fi
  local run_id
  run_id="$(basename "$file")"
  run_id="${run_id%.xml}"

  # The awk script concatenates the whole file into a single buffer, then
  # splits on the "<testcase " boundary so attributes and nested
  # failure/skipped elements can be inspected as a self-contained chunk.
  awk -v run_id="$run_id" '
    function extract(attr, str,   pat, val) {
      # Match ATTR="..." preceded by a non-alphanumeric char (so "classname"
      # does not shadow "name").
      pat = "(^|[^a-zA-Z0-9_])" attr "=\"[^\"]*\""
      if (match(str, pat)) {
        val = substr(str, RSTART, RLENGTH)
        sub(/^[^"]*"/, "", val)
        sub(/"$/, "", val)
        return val
      }
      return ""
    }
    { buf = buf " " $0 }
    END {
      suite = ""
      if (match(buf, /<testsuite[ \t\n][^>]*>/)) {
        header = substr(buf, RSTART, RLENGTH)
        suite = extract("name", header)
      }
      n = split(buf, chunks, /<testcase[ \t\n]/)
      for (i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
        tc = chunks[i]
        body = ""
        if ((e = index(tc, "</testcase>")) > 0) {
          body = substr(tc, 1, e - 1)
        } else if ((e = index(tc, "/>")) > 0) {
          body = substr(tc, 1, e - 1)
        } else {
          body = tc
        }
        name = extract("name", body)
        dur  = extract("time", body)
        if (dur == "") dur = "0"
        status = "passed"
        if (body ~ /<failure/ || body ~ /<error/) status = "failed"
        else if (body ~ /<skipped/)               status = "skipped"
        printf "%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\n", run_id, suite, name, status, dur
      }
    }
  ' "$file"
}

# <testcase ...>...<failure|error|skipped/></testcase>.
# run_id is derived from the file basename (extension stripped).
parse_junit() {
  local file="$1"
  if [[ ! -f "$file" ]]; then
    echo "parse_junit: file not found: $file" >&2
    return 1
  fi
  local run_id
  run_id="$(basename "$file")"
  run_id="${run_id%.xml}"

  # The awk script concatenates the whole file into a single buffer, then
  # splits on the "<testcase " boundary so attributes and nested
  # failure/skipped elements can be inspected as a self-contained chunk.
  awk -v run_id="$run_id" '
    function extract(attr, str,   pat, val) {
      # Match ATTR="..." preceded by a non-alphanumeric char (so "classname"
      # does not shadow "name").
      pat = "(^|[^a-zA-Z0-9_])" attr "=\"[^\"]*\""
      if (match(str, pat)) {
        val = substr(str, RSTART, RLENGTH)
        sub(/^[^"]*"/, "", val)
        sub(/"$/, "", val)
        return val
      }
      return ""
    }
    { buf = buf " " $0 }
    END {
      suite = ""
      if (match(buf, /<testsuite[ \t\n][^>]*>/)) {
        header = substr(buf, RSTART, RLENGTH)
        suite = extract("name", header)
      }
      n = split(buf, chunks, /<testcase[ \t\n]/)
      for (i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
        tc = chunks[i]
        body = ""
        if ((e = index(tc, "</testcase>")) > 0) {
          body = substr(tc, 1, e - 1)
        } else if ((e = index(tc, "/>")) > 0) {
          body = substr(tc, 1, e - 1)
        } else {
          body = tc
        }
        name = extract("name", body)
        dur  = extract("time", body)
        if (dur == "") dur = "0"
        status = "passed"
        if (body ~ /<failure/ || body ~ /<error/) status = "failed"
        else if (body ~ /<skipped/)               status = "skipped"
        printf "%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\n", run_id, suite, name, status, dur
      }
    }
  ' "$file"
}

# collect_results DIR
# Walk DIR (non-recursive) for *.xml and *.json files, dispatch to the
# appropriate parser, and emit the concatenated TSV stream on stdout.
# Exits 0 even when DIR has no matching files (empty stream is valid input
# for the downstream aggregation steps).
collect_results() {
  local dir="$1"
  if [[ ! -d "$dir" ]]; then
    echo "collect_results: directory not found: $dir" >&2
    return 1
  fi
  local f
  shopt -s nullglob
  for f in "$dir"/*.xml; do
    parse_junit "$f"
  done
  for f in "$dir"/*.json; do
    parse_json "$f"
  done
  shopt -u nullglob
}

generate_markdown() {
  local dir="$1"
  if [[ ! -d "$dir" ]]; then

    return 1
  fi

  local tmp
  tmp="$(mktemp)"
  collect_results "$dir" >"$tmp"

  local totals flaky
  totals="$(compute_totals <"$tmp")"
  flaky="$(compute_flaky <"$tmp")"
  rm -f "$tmp"

  local total passed failed skipped duration
  total=$(awk -v s="$totals" 'BEGIN { for (i=split(s,a," "); i>=1; i--) { split(a[i],kv,"="); if (kv[1]=="total") { print kv[2]; exit } } }')
  passed=$(awk -v s="$totals" 'BEGIN { for (i=split(s,a," "); i>=1; i--) { split(a[i],kv,"="); if (kv[1]=="passed") { print kv[2]; exit } } }')
  failed=$(awk -v s="$totals" 'BEGIN { for (i=split(s,a," "); i>=1; i--) { split(a[i],kv,"="); if (kv[1]=="failed") { print kv[2]; exit } } }')
  skipped=$(awk -v s="$totals" 'BEGIN { for (i=split(s,a," "); i>=1; i--) { split(a[i],kv,"="); if (kv[1]=="skipped") { print kv[2]; exit } } }')
  duration=$(awk -v s="$totals" 'BEGIN { for (i=split(s,a," "); i>=1; i--) { split(a[i],kv,"="); if (kv[1]=="duration") { print kv[2]; exit } } }')

  local status_line
  if [[ "${failed:-0}" -gt 0 ]]; then
    status_line="FAILED"
  elif [[ "${total:-0}" -eq 0 ]]; then
    status_line="NO_RESULTS"
  else
    status_line="PASSED"
  fi

  printf '# Test Results Summary\n\n'
  printf '**Status**: %s\n\n' "$status_line"
  printf '## Totals\n\n'
  printf '| Total | Passed | Failed | Skipped | Duration (s) |\n'
  printf '|-------|--------|--------|---------|--------------|\n'
  printf '| %s | %s | %s | %s | %s |\n\n' \
         "${total:-0}" "${passed:-0}" "${failed:-0}" "${skipped:-0}" "${duration:-0}"

  printf '## Flaky Tests\n\n'
  if [[ -z "$flaky" ]]; then

_main() {
  local output=""
  local dir=""
  while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do
    case "$1" in

      --output|-o) output="$2"; shift 2 ;;
      --)          shift; dir="$1"; break ;;
      -*)          echo "aggregate.sh: unknown option: $1" >&2; _usage >&2; return 2 ;;

  if [[ -z "$dir" ]]; then

  if [[ ! -d "$dir" ]]; then

  local totals

  local failed

  if [[ "${failed:-0}" -gt 0 ]]; then
    return 1
  fi
  return 0
}

#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# aggregate.sh — parse JUnit XML + JSON test result files, aggregate across
# multiple runs (matrix build), compute totals, flag flaky tests, and emit a
# GitHub-Actions-compatible markdown summary.
#
# Internal representation of a single result row is TSV with five fields:
#   run_id<TAB>suite<TAB>name<TAB>status<TAB>duration
# where status ∈ {passed, failed, skipped}.
#
# This file is both a library (when sourced) and a CLI (when executed).

set -euo pipefail

# parse_json FILE
# Reads a JSON test-result file and emits one TSV record per test.
# Expected schema: { "run_id": "...", "tests": [ { "suite": "...", "name":
# "...", "status": "...", "duration": 0.12 }, ... ] }
parse_json() {
  local file="$1"
  if [[ ! -f "$file" ]]; then
    echo "parse_json: file not found: $file" >&2
    return 1
  fi
  jq -r '
    (.run_id // ($__filename | sub("\\.json$"; ""))) as $rid
    | .tests[]
    | [$rid, (.suite // ""), .name, .status, ((.duration // 0) | tostring)]
    | @tsv
  ' --arg __filename "$(basename "$file")" "$file"
}

# parse_junit FILE
# Parse a JUnit-style XML test report and emit one TSV record per testcase.
# Supports <testcase .../> (self-closing, implicit pass) and
# <testcase ...>...<failure|error|skipped/></testcase>.
# run_id is derived from the file basename (extension stripped).
parse_junit() {
  local file="$1"
  if [[ ! -f "$file" ]]; then
    echo "parse_junit: file not found: $file" >&2
    return 1
  fi
  local run_id
  run_id="$(basename "$file")"
  run_id="${run_id%.xml}"

  # The awk script concatenates the whole file into a single buffer, then
  # splits on the "<testcase " boundary so attributes and nested
  # failure/skipped elements can be inspected as a self-contained chunk.
  awk -v run_id="$run_id" '
    function extract(attr, str,   pat, val) {
      # Match ATTR="..." preceded by a non-alphanumeric char (so "classname"
      # does not shadow "name").
      pat = "(^|[^a-zA-Z0-9_])" attr "=\"[^\"]*\""
      if (match(str, pat)) {
        val = substr(str, RSTART, RLENGTH)
        sub(/^[^"]*"/, "", val)
        sub(/"$/, "", val)
        return val
      }
      return ""
    }
    { buf = buf " " $0 }
    END {
      suite = ""
      if (match(buf, /<testsuite[ \t\n][^>]*>/)) {
        header = substr(buf, RSTART, RLENGTH)
        suite = extract("name", header)
      }
      n = split(buf, chunks, /<testcase[ \t\n]/)
      for (i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
        tc = chunks[i]
        body = ""
        if ((e = index(tc, "</testcase>")) > 0) {
          body = substr(tc, 1, e - 1)
        } else if ((e = index(tc, "/>")) > 0) {
          body = substr(tc, 1, e - 1)
        } else {
          body = tc
        }
        name = extract("name", body)
        dur  = extract("time", body)
        if (dur == "") dur = "0"
        status = "passed"
        if (body ~ /<failure/ || body ~ /<error/) status = "failed"
        else if (body ~ /<skipped/)               status = "skipped"
        printf "%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\n", run_id, suite, name, status, dur
      }
    }
  ' "$file"
}

# collect_results DIR
# Walk DIR (non-recursive) for *.xml and *.json files, dispatch to the
# appropriate parser, and emit the concatenated TSV stream on stdout.
# Exits 0 even when DIR has no matching files (empty stream is valid input
# for the downstream aggregation steps).
collect_results() {
  local dir="$1"
  if [[ ! -d "$dir" ]]; then
    echo "collect_results: directory not found: $dir" >&2
    return 1
  fi
  local f
  shopt -s nullglob
  for f in "$dir"/*.xml; do
    parse_junit "$f"
  done
  for f in "$dir"/*.json; do
    parse_json "$f"
  done
  shopt -u nullglob
}

# compute_totals
# Consume a TSV stream on stdin and emit five key=value lines:
#   total=...   (distinct suite+name pairs)
#   passed=...  failed=...   skipped=...
#   duration=... (sum of all row durations)
compute_totals() {
  awk -F'\t' '
    BEGIN { passed = 0; failed = 0; skipped = 0; duration = 0 }
    NF >= 5 {
      status = $4
      duration += $5 + 0
      key = $2 "\x1f" $3
      seen[key] = 1
      if (status == "passed")       passed++
      else if (status == "failed")  failed++
      else if (status == "skipped") skipped++
    }
    END {
      total = 0
      for (k in seen) total++
      # Trim trailing zeros to keep comparisons simple.
      dur = sprintf("%.6f", duration)
      sub(/0+$/, "", dur)
      sub(/\.$/, "", dur)
      if (dur == "" || dur == "-0") dur = "0"
      printf "total=%d passed=%d failed=%d skipped=%d duration=%s\n", \
             total, passed, failed, skipped, dur
    }
  '
}

# compute_flaky
# Consume a TSV stream on stdin and emit one TSV row per flaky test:
#   suite<TAB>name<TAB>pass_count<TAB>fail_count
# Flaky = (suite, name) saw at least one "passed" AND at least one "failed"
# across runs. Skipped statuses are ignored for the purposes of flakiness.
compute_flaky() {
  awk -F'\t' '
    NF >= 5 {
      key = $2 "\x1f" $3
      keys[key] = 1
      if ($4 == "passed") passes[key]++
      else if ($4 == "failed") fails[key]++
    }
    END {
      # Iterate in insertion order (gawk/mawk both preserve for seen arrays
      # in practice, but sort for determinism).
      n = 0
      for (k in keys) out[n++] = k
      # Simple bubble sort for stable, deterministic output.
      for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        for (j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
          if (out[j] < out[i]) { t = out[i]; out[i] = out[j]; out[j] = t }
        }
      }
      for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        k = out[i]
        p = passes[k] + 0
        f = fails[k] + 0
        if (p > 0 && f > 0) {
          split(k, parts, "\x1f")
          printf "%s\t%s\t%d\t%d\n", parts[1], parts[2], p, f
        }
      }
    }
  '
}

name: Test Results Aggregator

# The workflow exercises bin/aggregate.sh end-to-end in CI. The harness in
# tests/harness.bats drives it locally with act, swapping out the files in
# results/ per test case so the same pipeline emits different summaries.

on:
  push:
    branches: ["**"]
  pull_request:
  workflow_dispatch:

permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  unit-tests:
    name: Unit tests (bats)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Check out repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install bats and jq
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          if ! command -v bats >/dev/null 2>&1 || ! command -v jq >/dev/null 2>&1; then
            sudo apt-get update -y
            sudo apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends bats jq
          fi
          bats --version
          jq --version

      - name: Run unit tests
        run: bats tests/aggregate.bats

  aggregate:
    name: Aggregate test results
    needs: unit-tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Check out repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install jq
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          if ! command -v jq >/dev/null 2>&1; then
            sudo apt-get update -y
            sudo apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends jq
          fi
          jq --version

      - name: Prepare results directory
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          mkdir -p results
          # The external harness overlays case-specific fixtures into
          # results/ before invoking act. When the directory is empty
          # (e.g. a plain CI run) fall back to the bundled sample files
          # so the workflow is still self-demonstrable.
          if [ -z "$(ls -A results 2>/dev/null || true)" ]; then
            cp tests/fixtures/*.xml tests/fixtures/*.json results/
          fi
          echo "--- results/ contents ---"
          ls -la results

      - name: Aggregate and emit summary
        run: |
          set -uo pipefail
          # The script exits 1 when any test failed. That is expected for
          # some fixture sets — it reflects the aggregated test outcomes,
          # not a malfunction of the aggregator. Capture the exit code but
          # do NOT propagate it so the workflow still succeeds.
          bash bin/aggregate.sh results/ > summary.md
          agg_exit=$?
          echo "AGGREGATOR_EXIT=${agg_exit}"
          echo "--- summary.md ---"
          cat summary.md
          echo "--- end summary.md ---"
          cat summary.md >> "$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY"

      - name: Check out repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install bats and jq
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          if ! command -v bats >/dev/null 2>&1 || ! command -v jq >/dev/null 2>&1; then
            sudo apt-get update -y
            sudo apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends bats jq
          fi
          bats --version
          jq --version

      - name: Run unit tests
        run: bats tests/aggregate.bats

  aggregate:
    name: Aggregate test results
    needs: unit-tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Check out repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install jq
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          if ! command -v jq >/dev/null 2>&1; then
            sudo apt-get update -y
            sudo apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends jq
          fi
          jq --version

      - name: Prepare results directory
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          mkdir -p results
          # The external harness overlays case-specific fixtures into
          # results/ before invoking act. When the directory is empty
          # (e.g. a plain CI run) fall back to the bundled sample files
          # so the workflow is still self-demonstrable.
          if [ -z "$(ls -A results 2>/dev/null || true)" ]; then
            cp tests/fixtures/*.xml tests/fixtures/*.json results/
          fi
          echo "--- results/ contents ---"
          ls -la results

      - name: Aggregate and emit summary
        run: |
          set -uo pipefail
          # The script exits 1 when any test failed. That is expected for
          # some fixture sets — it reflects the aggregated test outcomes,
          # not a malfunction of the aggregator. Capture the exit code but
          # do NOT propagate it so the workflow still succeeds.
          bash bin/aggregate.sh results/ > summary.md
          agg_exit=$?
          echo "AGGREGATOR_EXIT=${agg_exit}"
          echo "--- summary.md ---"
          cat summary.md
          echo "--- end summary.md ---"
          cat summary.md >> "$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY"

#!/usr/bin/env bats
#
# End-to-end harness: runs the GitHub Actions workflow locally via `act`
# against three different fixture sets. For each case the harness
#   1. sets up a temp git repo containing this project + the case's
#      fixtures overlaid into results/
#   2. invokes `act push --rm`
#   3. appends all captured output to act-result.txt in the project root
#   4. asserts exit code 0 and that the expected summary values appear
#      verbatim in the captured output.
#
# act runs are expensive, so the three cases share a single bats file with
# one @test per case.

setup_file() {
  PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "${BATS_TEST_DIRNAME}/.." && pwd)"
  ACT_RESULT="${PROJECT_ROOT}/act-result.txt"
  export PROJECT_ROOT ACT_RESULT
  # Reset the result file at the start of the harness run.
  : >"$ACT_RESULT"
}

# Prepare a temp git repo that contains the project files plus the given
# case's fixture files overlaid into results/. Echoes the repo path.
_prepare_repo() {
  local case_dir="$1"
  local tmp
  tmp="$(mktemp -d)"

  # Copy project files (excluding .git, act-result.txt, and case fixtures
  # that aren't part of the case being tested).
  tar -C "$PROJECT_ROOT" \
      --exclude='./.git' \
      --exclude='./act-result.txt' \
      --exclude='./results' \
      -cf - . \
    | tar -C "$tmp" -xf -

  mkdir -p "$tmp/results"
  cp "$case_dir"/* "$tmp/results/"

  # act needs a git repo to infer event context.
  (
    cd "$tmp"
    git init -q
    git config user.email "harness@example.com"
    git config user.name  "harness"
    git add -A
    git commit -q -m "harness: case fixture"
  )
  echo "$tmp"
}

# Run act inside REPO_DIR and append output to act-result.txt with a
# clear delimiter. Echoes the act exit code.
_run_act() {
  local repo="$1"
  local label="$2"
  local out rc=0

  {
    echo "================================================================"
    echo "CASE: $label"
    echo "REPO: $repo"
    echo "================================================================"
  } >>"$ACT_RESULT"

  out="$(cd "$repo" && act push --rm 2>&1)" || rc=$?
  printf '%s\n' "$out" >>"$ACT_RESULT"
  {
    echo "----------------------------------------------------------------"
    echo "ACT_EXIT: $rc"
    echo "END CASE: $label"
    echo "================================================================"
    echo
  } >>"$ACT_RESULT"

  printf '%s\n' "$out"
  return "$rc"
}

@test "case1 (all green, mixed formats) runs through act with expected totals" {
  local repo; repo="$(_prepare_repo "$PROJECT_ROOT/tests/cases/case1")"
  run _run_act "$repo" "case1-all-green"
  rm -rf "$repo"

  # Assertions.
  [ "$status" -eq 0 ]
  # Every job must succeed (act prints "Job succeeded" per job).
  local jobs_ok
  jobs_ok=$(printf '%s\n' "$output" | grep -c 'Job succeeded')
  [ "$jobs_ok" -ge 2 ]
  # Unit-tests step ran all 23 bats tests.
  [[ "$output" == *"23 tests"* ]]
  [[ "$output" == *"Status"*"PASSED"* ]]
  # Totals row: total=2, passed=4, failed=0, skipped=0, duration=0.64.
  [[ "$output" == *"| 2 | 4 | 0 | 0 | 0.64 |"* ]]
  [[ "$output" == *"No flaky tests detected"* ]]
  [[ "$output" == *"AGGREGATOR_EXIT=0"* ]]
}

@test "case2 (failures + flaky) runs through act and reports flaky tests" {
  local repo; repo="$(_prepare_repo "$PROJECT_ROOT/tests/cases/case2")"
  run _run_act "$repo" "case2-flaky"
  rm -rf "$repo"

  [ "$status" -eq 0 ]
  local jobs_ok
  jobs_ok=$(printf '%s\n' "$output" | grep -c 'Job succeeded')
  [ "$jobs_ok" -ge 2 ]
  [[ "$output" == *"Status"*"FAILED"* ]]
  # Totals row: total=2, passed=2, failed=4, skipped=0, duration=1.02.
  [[ "$output" == *"| 2 | 2 | 4 | 0 | 1.02 |"* ]]
  # Flaky row: Core | test_a | 2 passes | 1 fail.
  [[ "$output" == *"| Core | test_a | 2 | 1 |"* ]]
  # Aggregator exits 1 because there are failures.
  [[ "$output" == *"AGGREGATOR_EXIT=1"* ]]
}

@test "case3 (skipped-heavy single file) runs through act" {
  local repo; repo="$(_prepare_repo "$PROJECT_ROOT/tests/cases/case3")"
  run _run_act "$repo" "case3-skipped"
  rm -rf "$repo"

  [ "$status" -eq 0 ]
  local jobs_ok
  jobs_ok=$(printf '%s\n' "$output" | grep -c 'Job succeeded')
  [ "$jobs_ok" -ge 2 ]
  [[ "$output" == *"Status"*"PASSED"* ]]
  # Totals row: total=3, passed=1, failed=0, skipped=2, duration=0.5.
  [[ "$output" == *"| 3 | 1 | 0 | 2 | 0.5 |"* ]]
  [[ "$output" == *"No flaky tests detected"* ]]
  [[ "$output" == *"AGGREGATOR_EXIT=0"* ]]
}

@test "act-result.txt exists and is non-empty" {
  [ -s "$ACT_RESULT" ]
}

content.text: Let me refactor: move act execution into a dedicated script and have bats validate the resulting file. This avoids re-running act on each bats run.

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# End-to-end driver: run the GitHub Actions workflow locally via `act` for
# every test case in tests/cases/*, saving all output (delimited by case) to
# act-result.txt in the project root. This is the "expensive" half of the
# harness; tests/harness.bats validates the captured output without
# re-invoking act.
#
# Usage:
#   tests/run-act-cases.sh            # run all cases
#   tests/run-act-cases.sh case1 ...  # run only the listed cases

set -euo pipefail

PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/.." && pwd)"
ACT_RESULT="${PROJECT_ROOT}/act-result.txt"

# Prepare a throwaway git repo that mirrors the project with one case's
# fixtures overlaid into results/. Echoes the repo path.
prepare_repo() {
  local case_dir="$1"
  local tmp
  tmp="$(mktemp -d)"

  tar -C "$PROJECT_ROOT" \
      --exclude='./.git' \
      --exclude='./act-result.txt' \
      --exclude='./results' \
      -cf - . \
    | tar -C "$tmp" -xf -

  mkdir -p "$tmp/results"
  cp "$case_dir"/* "$tmp/results/"

  (
    cd "$tmp"
    git init -q
    git config user.email "harness@example.com"
    git config user.name  "harness"
    git add -A
    git commit -q -m "harness: case fixture"
  )
  echo "$tmp"
}

# Run act for one case and append the captured output (including a
# structured exit marker) to act-result.txt.
run_case() {
  local case_name="$1"
  local case_dir="${PROJECT_ROOT}/tests/cases/${case_name}"
  if [[ ! -d "$case_dir" ]]; then
    echo "run-act-cases: no such case: $case_name" >&2
    return 2
  fi

  local repo rc=0
  repo="$(prepare_repo "$case_dir")"

  {
    echo "================================================================"
    echo "CASE: ${case_name}"
    echo "REPO: ${repo}"
    echo "================================================================"
  } >>"$ACT_RESULT"

  (cd "$repo" && act push --rm 2>&1) >>"$ACT_RESULT" || rc=$?

  {
    echo "----------------------------------------------------------------"
    echo "ACT_EXIT: $rc"
    echo "END CASE: ${case_name}"
    echo "================================================================"
    echo
  } >>"$ACT_RESULT"

  rm -rf "$repo"
  return "$rc"
}

main() {
  : >"$ACT_RESULT"   # truncate on each full run
  local cases=("$@")
  if [[ ${#cases[@]} -eq 0 ]]; then
    local d
    for d in "${PROJECT_ROOT}/tests/cases"/*/; do
      cases+=("$(basename "$d")")
    done
  fi
  local overall=0
  local c
  for c in "${cases[@]}"; do
    echo "=== running act for ${c} ==="
    if ! run_case "$c"; then
      echo "act failed for ${c} (exit $?)" >&2
      overall=1
    fi
  done
  return "$overall"
}

main "$@"

#!/usr/bin/env bats
#
# End-to-end harness: runs the GitHub Actions workflow locally via `act`
# against three different fixture sets. For each case the harness
#   1. sets up a temp git repo containing this project + the case's
#      fixtures overlaid into results/
#   2. invokes `act push --rm`
#   3. appends all captured output to act-result.txt in the project root
#   4. asserts exit code 0 and that the expected summary values appear
#      verbatim in the captured output.
#
# act runs are expensive, so the three cases share a single bats file with
# one @test per case.

setup_file() {
  PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "${BATS_TEST_DIRNAME}/.." && pwd)"
  ACT_RESULT="${PROJECT_ROOT}/act-result.txt"
  export PROJECT_ROOT ACT_RESULT
  # Reset the result file at the start of the harness run.
  : >"$ACT_RESULT"
}

# Prepare a temp git repo that contains the project files plus the given
# case's fixture files overlaid into results/. Echoes the repo path.
_prepare_repo() {
  local case_dir="$1"
  local tmp
  tmp="$(mktemp -d)"

  # Copy project files (excluding .git, act-result.txt, and case fixtures
  # that aren't part of the case being tested).
  tar -C "$PROJECT_ROOT" \
      --exclude='./.git' \
      --exclude='./act-result.txt' \
      --exclude='./results' \
      -cf - . \
    | tar -C "$tmp" -xf -

  mkdir -p "$tmp/results"
  cp "$case_dir"/* "$tmp/results/"

  # act needs a git repo to infer event context.
  (
    cd "$tmp"
    git init -q
    git config user.email "harness@example.com"
    git config user.name  "harness"
    git add -A
    git commit -q -m "harness: case fixture"
  )
  echo "$tmp"
}

# Run act inside REPO_DIR and append output to act-result.txt with a
# clear delimiter. Echoes the act exit code.
_run_act() {
  local repo="$1"
  local label="$2"
  local out rc=0

  {
    echo "================================================================"
    echo "CASE: $label"
    echo "REPO: $repo"
    echo "================================================================"
  } >>"$ACT_RESULT"

  out="$(cd "$repo" && act push --rm 2>&1)" || rc=$?
  printf '%s\n' "$out" >>"$ACT_RESULT"
  {
    echo "----------------------------------------------------------------"
    echo "ACT_EXIT: $rc"
    echo "END CASE: $label"
    echo "================================================================"
    echo
  } >>"$ACT_RESULT"

  printf '%s\n' "$out"
  return "$rc"
}

@test "case1 (all green, mixed formats) runs through act with expected totals" {
  local repo; repo="$(_prepare_repo "$PROJECT_ROOT/tests/cases/case1")"
  run _run_act "$repo" "case1-all-green"
  rm -rf "$repo"

  # Assertions.
  [ "$status" -eq 0 ]
  # Every job must succeed (act prints "Job succeeded" per job).
  local jobs_ok
  jobs_ok=$(printf '%s\n' "$output" | grep -c 'Job succeeded')
  [ "$jobs_ok" -ge 2 ]
  # Unit-tests step ran all 23 bats tests.
  [[ "$output" == *"23 tests"* ]]
  [[ "$output" == *"Status"*"PASSED"* ]]
  # Totals row: total=2, passed=4, failed=0, skipped=0, duration=0.64.
  [[ "$output" == *"| 2 | 4 | 0 | 0 | 0.64 |"* ]]
  [[ "$output" == *"No flaky tests detected"* ]]
  [[ "$output" == *"AGGREGATOR_EXIT=0"* ]]
}

@test "case2 (failures + flaky) runs through act and reports flaky tests" {
  local repo; repo="$(_prepare_repo "$PROJECT_ROOT/tests/cases/case2")"
  run _run_act "$repo" "case2-flaky"
  rm -rf "$repo"

  [ "$status" -eq 0 ]
  local jobs_ok
  jobs_ok=$(printf '%s\n' "$output" | grep -c 'Job succeeded')
  [ "$jobs_ok" -ge 2 ]
  [[ "$output" == *"Status"*"FAILED"* ]]
  # Totals row: total=2, passed=2, failed=4, skipped=0, duration=1.02.
  [[ "$output" == *"| 2 | 2 | 4 | 0 | 1.02 |"* ]]
  # Flaky row: Core | test_a | 2 passes | 1 fail.
  [[ "$output" == *"| Core | test_a | 2 | 1 |"* ]]
  # Aggregator exits 1 because there are failures.
  [[ "$output" == *"AGGREGATOR_EXIT=1"* ]]
}

@test "case3 (skipped-heavy single file) runs through act" {
  local repo; repo="$(_prepare_repo "$PROJECT_ROOT/tests/cases/case3")"
  run _run_act "$repo" "case3-skipped"
  rm -rf "$repo"

  [ "$status" -eq 0 ]
  local jobs_ok
  jobs_ok=$(printf '%s\n' "$output" | grep -c 'Job succeeded')
  [ "$jobs_ok" -ge 2 ]
  [[ "$output" == *"Status"*"PASSED"* ]]
  # Totals row: total=3, passed=1, failed=0, skipped=2, duration=0.5.
  [[ "$output" == *"| 3 | 1 | 0 | 2 | 0.5 |"* ]]
  [[ "$output" == *"No flaky tests detected"* ]]
  [[ "$output" == *"AGGREGATOR_EXIT=0"* ]]
}

@test "act-result.txt exists and is non-empty" {
  [ -s "$ACT_RESULT" ]
}
